story_title: On the Move as a Peat
story_summary: A newly made partner at Peat Marwick asserts ownership by pushing for the removal of a negligent partner and later accepts an invitation to establish a new office in Orlando after securing a visit with his wife.

context.stakes: Firm reputation, client engagements (e.g., Cal-Maine Foods), internal partnership stability

core_conflict: Whether to act on observed partner negligence that endangered clients and the firm despite partnership norms protecting partners from dismissal.

decision_moments.trigger: negligence of the designated partner on the Cal-Maine Foods engagement
constraints: the man had been his partner for more than twenty years
firing a partner was an extremely rare event for Peat Marwick
Only Walter Hanson ... handled forced resignations of partners
options: Remain silent and continue managing the client responsibilities myself
Privately complain but avoid forcing formal action
Press senior leadership to remove the negligent partner and escalate to the executive committee
decision_moments.choice: Pressed senior leadership; persuaded Bill Norton to recommend removal to the executive committee with supporting testimony.
decision_moments.reasoning: As a partner I felt I now had the imprimatur to make my views known and believed the partner's conduct was egregious, grossly unprofessional, reckless and put the firm at risk.
decision_moments.risk_accepted: Confronting long-standing colleagues and triggering an uncommon forced resignation that could strain internal relationships
decision_moments.outcome: Bill Norton made a formal recommendation; Walter Hanson accepted and the partner was removed.

decision_moments.trigger: the firm had decided to open an office in Orlando and invited me to move there
constraints: desire to consider family's interests (wife's view and readiness)
executive committee had already decided I was the best person to do this
options: Accept the assignment immediately without a visit
Accept but first visit Orlando with my wife to confirm suitability
decision_moments.choice: Agreed to relocate but insisted on visiting Orlando with his wife first; then accepted the assignment.
decision_moments.reasoning: Wanted to be sure the move was right for his wife and family before committing
decision_moments.risk_accepted: Commitment to establish and lead a new office in a different city
decision_moments.outcome: He agreed to move and prepared to go to Orlando in August 1971.

principles.principle: Assume ownership and use formal authority to address previously suppressed problems.
principles.evidence: I took on the role of ownership with sincerity and earnestness.

principles.principle: Raise concerns about professional negligence when it endangers clients and the firm.
principles.evidence: I strongly believed that the partner should be evicted from the firm.

principles.principle: Leverage formal position to make previously withheld views known.
principles.evidence: As a partner myself, however, I felt that I now had the imprimatur to make my views known.

principles.principle: Include family considerations in major relocation decisions.
principles.evidence: I said I would like to take my wife and make a visit to Orlando to be sure it was the right thing for us.

decision_heuristics.heuristic: When promoted to formal authority, speak up about critical issues you previously withheld.
decision_heuristics.evidence: As a partner myself, however, I felt that I now had the imprimatur to make my views known.

decision_heuristics.heuristic: Persistently press leadership until action is taken when conduct endangers clients or the firm.
decision_heuristics.evidence: I pressed hard on the matter.

decision_heuristics.heuristic: When offered an important relocation, involve and consult your spouse by visiting together before committing.
decision_heuristics.evidence: I said I would like to take my wife and make a visit to Orlando to be sure it was the right thing for us.

leadership_patterns.risk_tolerance: Willing to accept interpersonal and career risks to protect firm and clients
leadership_patterns.communication_style: Direct and assertive; confronts issues openly once authorized
leadership_patterns.accountability_posture: Proactive ownership; assumes responsibility for client and office outcomes
leadership_patterns.ethical_framing: Frames decisions around professional standards, client safety, and firm integrity

quotes.quote: I took on the role of ownership with sincerity and earnestness.
I strode into Bill Norton’s office and told him I strongly believed that the partner should be evicted from the firm.
As a partner myself, however, I felt that I now had the imprimatur to make my views known.
I said I would like to take my wife and make a visit to Orlando to be sure it was the right thing for us.
